title: Fixing CSS Flexbox Alignment with Variable Items

statement.markdown: Given a CSS stylesheet with a flex container, write a function that takes a string of CSS properties as input and returns the corrected CSS properties string with the `align-items` property set to `center` when the number of items in the container is odd. Otherwise, return the original string.
statement.inputFormat: A string of CSS properties, e.g., `display: flex; justify-content: space-between; align-items: `
statement.outputFormat: The corrected CSS properties string with the `align-items` property set to `center` when the number of items is odd, otherwise the original string
statement.notes: The input string may contain multiple CSS properties, and the number of items in the container may vary.

constraints.description: Number of items in the flex container

examples.output: display: flex; justify-content: space-between; align-items: center;
examples.explanation: The number of items is 5 (odd), so the `align-items` property should be set to `center`.

examples.input: display: flex; justify-content: space-between; align-items: flex-start;
examples.output: display: flex; justify-content: space-between; align-items: flex-start;
examples.explanation: The number of items is 4 (even), so the `align-items` property should remain `flex-start`.

hints.text: Check the length of the input string to determine the number of items in the container.
Use the modulo operator to check if the number of items is odd or even.

editorial.title: Editorial: Fixing CSS Flexbox Alignment with Variable Items
editorial.intuition: The key to solving this problem is to check the length of the input string, which represents the CSS properties, to determine the number of items in the container. Then, use the modulo operator to check if the number of items is odd or even.
editorial.bruteForce: One possible approach is to manually count the number of items in the container and then set the `align-items` property accordingly.
editorial.betterSolution: A more efficient approach is to use the modulo operator to check if the number of items is odd or even, and then set the `align-items` property accordingly.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is to use a simple if-else statement to check the number of items and set the `align-items` property accordingly.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness is that the optimal solution correctly sets the `align-items` property to `center` when the number of items is odd and leaves it unchanged when the number of items is even.
editorial.dryRun: Here is a step-by-step example run: Input string = `display: flex; justify-content: space-between; align-items: `. Number of items = 5 (odd). `align-items` property should be set to `center`. Output string = `display: flex; justify-content: space-between; align-items: center;`.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to count the number of items in the container

testCases.output: display: flex; justify-content: space-between; align-items: center;

testCases.input: display: flex; justify-content: space-between; align-items: flex-start;
testCases.output: display: flex; justify-content: space-between; align-items: flex-start;
